SALT LAKE CITY, 			JULY 15, 1887.


INDEPENDENCE DAY.

	The celebration on the 4th of July made quite
a display on the streets,and in Liberty Park where
the people assembled, everything went off satis-
factorily to all. The procession was the great
feature, hundreds of people crowded the sidewalks
in carriages and on foot to see the parade, and all
appeared to enjoy the music and the sight-seeing
and to appreciate the occasion to its utmost. At
the head of the procession was Col. Page, Mar-
shall of the day with a cavalcade of horsemen.
Held’s band came next playing national music;
following the band were carriages containing offi-
cials Gov. West, Ex-Gov. Murray, Chief Justice
Zane, Secretary Hall, James H. Moyle Esq. The
Utah Commission, County officers and members
of the Constitutional Convention and other promi-
nent men and citizens. Then came Capt. Bishop
and a number of horsemen,the City Band Knights
of Pythias in full dress uniform, and next the
Colonial Car with the Goddess of Liberty, Miss
Celia Swenson personating the Goddess, and
thirteen other young girls representing the thir-
teen original states; the car was gorgously orna-
mented and sailors and fairies in costume at the
four corners gave it a very handsome appearance.
Another elaborately trimmed car followed, filled
with young ladies who each carried a streamer
having on it a name of one of the states of the
Union. Next came the Fire Brigade and then
the Base Ball Clubs all in uniform and the Loco
motive Engineers; these made quite a display with
their bright gay uniforms. Next in the parade
was the car filled with young ladies, designated
the Bouquet of Beauty. After this the trade dis-
plays and manufacturers fifteen or twenty vehicles
and machines. The showing in these was excel-
lent and they were arranged in good taste, with
banners, mottoes etc. emblematic of what they
represented. The last part of the procession
was styled the Antiques and Horribles, compris-
ing the absurd,humorous and oddities interspersed
very rarely with the beautiful, all, however, help-
ing to make variety to suit the taste of all who
congregated to witness the parade and celebrate
the day.
	On the Grand Stand at the Park the Federal
Officials and officers of the day,city and county offi-
cers and citizens were seated, the assembly was
called to order and first addressed by Gen. Eli H.
Murray; Prayer by Rev.T.C.Iliff; John M. Young
read the Declaration of Independence and the
band played “The Star Spangled Banner." Gov. 
West then delivered an oration; after he had con-
cluded his address T. E. Harper sung the Star
Spangled Banner; the next speech was from
Judge Zane,followed by one from James H. Moyle;
the band then played some soul stirring music and
the anthem America was rendered in an effective
and impressive manner by G. D. Pyper, M. J.
Thomas, Bessie Dean and Maggie Hull. This
completed the programme when the benediction
was prononnced by Rev. Ilift. This closed the
proceedings of the day at the Park.


﻿There was the usual applause and cheering and
so forth, and on the whole the day was an agreea-
ble one to all interested. The zeal and en-
thusiasm over Independence Day never dies out
in the heart of an American, no matter what
their position in life or circumstances may be. It
is a part of their nature born in them and inherited
from generation to generation.
   The display of fire works on Arsenal Hill in
the evening is said to have been the very best and
finest as well as most costly ever seen in this city.
There were very fine displays at several residences
in various parts of town and altogether the day
was a most enjoyable one.


[Column 1]
EDITORIAL THOUGHTS. 

	The Constitutional Convention which met on 
June 30, adjourned on the 6th of July having com-
pleted the work undertaken by its members, viz., 
the framing of a Constitution to be submitted to 
the vote of the people and afterwards presented to 
the President and Congress of the United States
asking that Utah be admitted into the Union of 
States. This is the sixth Constitution framed and 
will be the sixth time the people of Utah have
sought to obtain a state government. The people
are entitled to recognition and no good reason can
be urged against the admission of this Territory. 
Everything is favorable so far as the condition 
of the Territory itself is concerned for it is abun-
dantly able to take upon itself the added responsi-
bility of statehood. 
	The new Constitution adopted by the Convention 
and which will undoubtedly be ratified by the peo-
ple at the coming election in August is in strict
conformity with the laws and regulations of other
states. What pretext any fair-minded person can 
conjure up against it, it is impossible at this time 
to imagine. It remains, however, to be proven
what will be done with the new Constitution 
if it is ratified by the people. It will certainly 
be objectionable to a certain class of men, who 
have made and are still trying to make so much
capital out of Mormonism as a hobby. One would
naturally think the topic had worn threadbare
long ago, but there are those who seem to think
they can gain notoriety, money and fame by "ex-
posing Mormonistm," as they call it, as if after all 
these years of half a century and more the people
had not become accustomed to the stale stories
circulated and reiterated so often. If congress
should happen to settle this matter of statehood
by admitting Utah and she should have the
opportunity of electing men to the highest offices
in the gift of the people or exercise a little home
rule one cannot help wondering what will become 
of some of the men, who for so many years have
followed the occupation of exposing Mormonism
to Congress and the fifty millions of people in the
United States. They will certainly take up a new
phase of the question or adopt some other method
for getting a living. And then there are the anti-
Mormon women, who have become known as 
writers of sensational anti Mormon literature, and
delivering lectures on the "Mormon Monster" and
kindred topics, what will they find to talk about
and write about after having found that all their
well laid schemes to keep Utah and the Mormons
out of the Union have failed. We shall see in 
good time. They have fretted and worried them-
selves a great deal over the affairs of the Mor-
mons, strange, too, when one considers how well 
satisfied they are to live next door to those "poor, 
degraded, ignorant creatures." One would think 
they would prefer to go elsewhere, but no, they 
are glad enough to settle down on the land that
the Mormons redeemed from the desert, and sub-
dued making it habitable; "this country belongs
to the United States and should be for the loyal citi-
zens." etc.,etc.;this is the sort of language used by 
one of the great men of the nation; well, the Mor-
mons belong to the United States the majority of 

[Column 2]
the citizens of this fair Territory were born under
the stars and stripes, many were born here in 
Utah and ought to be entitled to some privileges
and rights, and therefore there is little need to talk
about taking away the country for somebody else
while the veritable owners who have bought and
paid for their lands and built their homes are in 
possession.
	It is, perhaps, very fortunate for the Mormons
that there are some sensible people left yet, even
among the most noted men in the United States
who think this people have rights that ought to be 
respected and are not afraid to say so. But with
the Lord on the side of the Latter-day Saints, and
they feel fully assured of that fact, what cause
have they to fear? None whatever while they
walk according to the teachings and instructions 
He has given unto them through His servants
who preside over His Church upon the earth. 
Let come what will come, it will all work together
for the good of His people inthe end; and no man, 
or body of men, or governments, or principalities, 
or powers can hinder or stop the progress of the
Gospel upon the earth, for this Gospel, the same
Gospel the Savior taught, the Gospel preached by 
the "Mormon" Elders, so called, shall be preached
unto all nations and then shall the end come. And
all nations shall then acknowledge God and
His power and authority, for it is said, "every
knee shall bow and every tongue confess" etc. 
	This people are not staking their all upon be-
coming a state, as some of the outside seem to 
think, no indeed. The Latter-day Saints place
their trust in one who orders all things, and who 
will deal out even-handed justice to all, and 
whether the President and Congress and people
of these United States are willing to admit Utah
as a State or not will make no difference with the
work of God established in these last days, never
to be thrown down or given to another people.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
	WE invite the young and the old and the middle
aged to contribute to the paper, giving the benefit 
of their best thoughts and ideas to those who
read, that it may be even more fully the EXPON-
ENT of the views and opinions of the Church of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. The paper is
circulated far and wide, and we want it to contain
the best and truest principles, clearly and vividly 
set forth, that it may be a missionary in the homes
of the Saints, and wherever it may find a place
abroad among the people of the world, or in the
hands of the tourist and "the stranger within our
gates," of whom, in these days, there are a multi-
tude from time to time.
	REPORTS of conferences from the Relief Society
of Bear Lake Stake and the R. S.
and Y. L. M. I. A. of Juab Stake, and of the
district conference of Relief Society held at Wells-
ville, will appear in our next issue of the paper.
We have some verses and other articles on hand,
that will appear in good time, and some not exactly 
suitable for our little paper, but which contain ex-
cellent thoughts suited to the occasion for which
they were written and read. The Primary Band
in Richmond sent us the verses written for them;
these will be printed on slips of paper and dis-
tributed to the little folks, and in this way we feel
sure they will be more acceptable, and do more
good to the little fellows than if published in the
EXPONENT.
	THERE have been some material changes made
recently in the Deseret Hospital, which it is hoped
and believed will be an advantage to the institution.
For some time past there has not been a surgeon
or physician resident in the house, but the Board
of Directors have secured the services of Dr. Ro-
mania B. Pratt, well known in the city and terri-
tory as a skillful practitioner, and she has been
duly installed as Resident Physician there, and
will have entire charge of the Hospital in all its


departments for the present, although it is the in-
tention, also, sometime in the near future, to have
a Medical Superintendent, but there is a good
strong corps of visiting physicians, who are always
ready and willing when called upon or assistance
is required, to come and do all in their power.
The Board feel very grateful to these surgeons
and physicians, both men and women, who have,
from time to time, attended operations and so
forth, and thus given time and valuable services
and influence to the Hospital, which is still, it may
be said, in its infancy, and has had rather a hard
struggle to keep up, but there seems a brighter
prospect at present. There are many of the
members of the Deseret Hospital Association
that have not yet paid the annual fee of member-
ship, and the President and Board of Directors
would be very glad if at this particular time, when
they are greatly in need of means, those whose
dues are not paid, would come forward and pay
or send the money to the Secretary, Mrs. E. B.
Wells, Office WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake
City, Utah. New memberships of either brethren
or sisters, are also earnestly solicited to assist in
this benevolent work and help sustain an institu-
tion where the sick and afflicted may be properly
nursed, cared for and treated by those who have
the spirit and influence of the Gospel.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S SPHERE.
	The word sphere, although relating to a limited
space, has quite an extensive meaning. The sun 
is called a sphere, and rightly, too; the planets
which comprise our solar system are spheres also,
and therefore the earth we occupy is a sphere.
The word also means, the circuit of action, of
knowledge, influence or power; having its bounds
and limits. Were it possible to step off the sur-
face of the earth into space, it might be said that
we had passed beyond this sphere, but we all
know this would be utterly impossible, hence we 
are confined to the circumference of the earth and
cannot go beyond it, at least not in our mortal 
state.
	Our existence, however, is a dual one. For
while the mortal part is limited to this earth and
cannot get beyond it, yet the immortal part can
reach out into space, can gaze upon and handle
with as much reality and comprehend with as
much certainty some things which we term
ethereal, as anything that we know naturally as
pertaining to this earth upon which we dwell. Yet
we are for the time being limited in our knowledge
as well as in our powers. While we are in the
midst of eternity, yet our progress in the knowl-
edge of eternal things is necessarily limited and
bounds are set from time to time limiting us in
our eternal progress within certain spheres, beyond
which for the time being we cannot pass no mat-
ter what our aspirations, or desires may be.
	Each woman possesses a separate and distinct
identity that cannot become any part of 
another identity. This identity is des-
tined to be preserved intact through all
the eternities that are to come and has
within it the elements of adaptation and fit-
ness to all conditions, both as pertaining to the
present as well as to the future. As we said we
are dual beings; yet is woman’s physical organism
—and although peculiar to herself—in most beauti-
ful harmony with her spiritual being. We are
speaking now of the entire race of womankind for
while individuals may be in some respects dissimi-
lar yet as a whole each individual very strongly
represents the other and it is not the single indi-
vidual that we are considering but woman as a 
whole.
	Although closely allied to man—and anomalous 
as it may appear—yet is her world or sphere dis-
tinct from his, as is plainly manifest in her entire 
life and being. Her desires, her hopes, her am-
[Column Two]
bitions, if at all correct, are decidedly womanly;
and when she assumes anything—either in speech
or conduct—that does not legitimately belong to
her she unsexes herself, and that, too, in the
ratio of such departure and is therefore to that ex-
tent outside of her proper sphere.
	Poets have made of woman an angel, while
satirists among many other things have declared
her to be an engima, neither of which is essentially
correct. Revelation teaches that there are elements
within her, which, when properly developed, will
take her far beyond any angelic condition or sphere,
while her true self is as plainly manifest as any
other fact when properly demonstrated.
	It is in the union of the sexes or the uniting of
woman with man, where the true study of woman
commences; all the other periods of her life are
simply preparatory to this grand climax of her
being and her completeness is not assured until
this goal is reached. Physical maturity may be
attained, it is true and so far we may justifiably
claim womanhood, but she is still lacking in com-
pleteness and this lack will always continue out-
side the marriage relation even should she live to
be a century old in this life, while in the future it
will still be more keenly felt. This fact as far as 
relates to this life is recognized the world over,
among the barbarians as well as the civilized; for
the spinster is generally made the butt of ridicule
and is often held in more or less contempt.
Woman's true sphere then, is wifehood and mother-
hood and everything pertaining to, or growing out
of these conditions is her natural element. How-
ever improved these conditions may become in
the future—as improved they will be—woman’s
advancement is dependent still upon these two 
conditions and is inseparably connected therewith
unless indeed she should choose to be an angel,
which is a condition very far below her true
sphere.
	There is incorporated in these two conditions
everything pertaining to her advancement, her
future queenly power, as well as a fulness of joy 
for evermore. In short, all that woman could de-
sire or crave or could possibly enjoy in the eter-
nities that are to come is incorporated and em-
bodied in these two conditions, while all her hopes
are centered therein and her fears and dreads in
this mortal life are as inseparably connected there-
with.
	The clamor that is made in the world to day
concerning "WOMAN'S RIGHTS" is in many
instances unwise and at the best relates to matters
that have but a feather’s weight as compared with
those all important interests involved in her being, 
and are unquestionably the result of a lack of 
true knowledge concerning herself. The all ab-
sorbing question abroad is, "Shall woman have
the right of franchise?" This is advocated with
such earnestness and zeal that we are almost led
to think that her whole existence and well being
depended upon this alone, while here in Utah with
the franchise taken away from us by Congress
after having possessed it for years, we make but
little ado, although we fully recognize the wrong
that has been thus inflicted upon us. Give woman
the franchise? Most certainly. In every State
and Territory of the Union as well as in every
part of the civilized world if she desires it and 
we declare that the ballot in the hands of women
would be as safe, as it now is in the hands of man
exclusively, and a little safer. But for the women
of Christendom to enter the political arena at this
time when cunning, chicanery, fraud and corrup-
tion flourish, would necessitate a cleansing first of
the Augean Stables which they would be prompted 
to attempt although the task would be an Hercu-
lean one. And all the world knows that woman's
faculty for removing dirt and filth is proverbial.
But there are a few things that some women have
advocated that appear to me to be altogether out
of place. For instance, a woman would make a 
very poor marshal, sheriff, or policeman, or con-
[Column Three]
stable; unless indeed the men who are liable to
arrest should willingly accompany her to a court
of justice or to jail, for her physical inferiority is
not adequate to this task, unless we except those
female Amazons that are only few in number and
for which we ought to feel very thankful.
	Neither is the judgment seat or the jury box any
part of woman's true sphere for besides many 
potent reasons that might be named; that stern
justice so necessary to be so often administered
seems to me altogether foreign to woman's nature
and the weighing of evidence, too,—often so very
conflicting—should, I think, be left to man whose
philosophical turn of mind admirably fits him for
both of these positions. I am not considering at
this time those corruptions and wrongs so often
exhibited by man, which might be elaborated at
great length but his very natural fitness for these
positions as against woman and vice versa.
	Woman makes a most excellent tutor of youth
and her services in a school board are of the
greatest value, while her very natural adaptation
to the sick room suggests that a thorough knowl-
edge of remedial agencies would make her all the
more efficient in this regard. From time imme-
morial has woman also been recognized as the 
most fitting and proper person to officiate in peri-
ods of accouchement which position involves a
most thorough and complete study of the science
of physiology, together with some knowledge of
anatomy; for ignorance in this regard becomes in
many instances criminal; human life being often
sacrificed in consequence. But as to woman's 
adaptability to become a surgeon in general has
always been a question in my mind. This I think
more legitimately belongs to the sterner sex. 
Those cases wherein woman has excelled in this
regard are the exception rather than the rule.
	We may claim, and perhaps justly too, that
woman's opportunities for the development of this
knowledge and skill have been very limited in the 
past, but the answer to this would be, that had
this been within woman's legitimate sphere she
surely would at some period during the past six
thousand years made exhibitions of the gift and
in so pronounced a manner, too, as to stamp her 
in this one instance at least the compeer of man,
for nature will show itself despite all obstacles.
	Now, my sisters, you cannot acuse me of selfish-
ness but must award to me that fairness which
I have sought to give this subject. Although I 
have been very concise and have noticed only a 
very few points among the many that might be 
named, I have not taken an abstract view of the
question but a general one and again assert, that I
firmly believe the legitimate sphere of woman is
ample for the development of all her powers, her
gifts and her natural endowments, and that within
these bounds can be found all the pleasures and
enjoyments necessary to her well being both here
and hereafter, and when the duties pertaining 
thereto shall have been fully performed we shall
arrive at that degree of fulness that constitutes
eternal felicity, and all within the circumference
of our own sphere. Therefore, WOMAN KNOW
THYSELF. 
							AUNT RUTH.


TIMELY REFLECTIONS.
	Aslant the misty morning air,through glittering 
circlets of rainbow hue, comes the glorious orb of
day peeping o’er yon snow-capped mountain peak,
casting its brilliant sheen o’er the teeming valley
below,where myriads of happy songsters are pour-
ing forth their glad welcome in a grand concert of
tuneful melody. Nature is all alive in radiant,
peaceful beauty, the discordant sounds of humani-
ty are hushed, the fragrance of bloom, the sound
of gently rushing waters, the glittering dewdrops
fill my senses with delight, and from the harmony
of sight and sound, my spirit gathers renewed
strength and buoyancy, and from my inmost soul


springs forth an anthem of thanksgiving and
praise for my birthplace, Zion, in whose develop-
ment and progress my greatest desires are cen-
tered. “Her people are my people, her God my
God'’, and under the direction of God’s inspired
servants all my labors shall be in union with those
who seek to establish Zion in righteousness. To
this end a still small voice whispers, “Neglect not
the gift within thee,” faint and undeveloped
though it be, its avenues of expression cramped
and crippled through lack of training and use and
the meagre facilities afforded for education before
the “desert blosomed like the rose," still a sense of
duty is urging on to action and endeavor. Thus
am I seated for a chat with the dear sisterhood of
Latter-day Saints, noble, intelligent, faithful and
God-fearing as I know them to be “with Improve-
ment our Motto and Perfection our Aim,” let us
reason together on subjects of mutual interest
and benefit. Freedom of thought, speech and the
press are still ours, though freedom of action and
expression at the polls has been most cruelly and
unjustly torn from us by the rulers of the great
nation, who so proudly boast of their honor and
chivalry to woman. Oh mockery! Does the
safety of a people or nation depend on the vote of
a few thousand women! Then indeed is the situa-
tion pitiable. Surely the grand structure of Ameri-
can independence must be tottering when Con-
gress enacts laws depriving women of the ballot
which they for 17 years exercised in an irreproach-
able manner. Truly we are the “down-trodden
women of Utah’' when the heads of our nation
conspire to oppress us. Still we rejoice in that
freedom wherewith Christ hath made us free; the
principles of the Gospel so despised and fought
against by the wicked give to woman this greatest
possible freedom, and will elevate her to the high-
est pinnacle of honor and refinement, for man is
not without the woman in the Lord and under
God’s laws their interest and advancement are
equal, “male and female created He them,” and
the Gospel surrounds women with a dignity and
importance undreamed of by Emperors, Kings or
presidents, and which they have no power to be-
stow, and while we are deprived of the right to
sustain by our vote those whom we have proved to
be our true friends in the past, we can still wield
an influence for the right by teaching our children
—our sons the importance of sustaining the con-
stitution of our country and every principle of
liberty, we can teach them to abhor the spirit of
bigotry and oppression—that prompted the gross
insult perpetrated upon their mothers, and like the
Nephite women, spoken of in the Book of Mor-
mon whose husbands were under oath not to shed
blood—we can inspire our sons (many  of whose
fathers are now bound by an odious test oath
from wielding the power of the franchise and
whose mothers are disfranchised without the cere-
mony of a hearing) with faith and resolution to
step forward and exercise the rights left them and
preserve the government of our fair territory from-
from being usurped by the clamorous minority
bent upon crushing out of existence those who
have made this country habitable to them.
	Our experience in the past, particularly the past
three years, has not inspired us with an over
amount of confidence in the justice or mercy of
our political rulers and the heartless Federal
Officials sent by them to administer their cruel
and oppressive laws. The Women of Utah—Lat-
ter-day Saints—have suffered too much from this
class to throw any power or influence in their
possession in favor of those who have proven
themselves to be their enemies—knowing this
the brave (?) minority have beseiged Congress to
strike the death blow at the power so much feared,
the ballot in the hands of a few thousand women.
And to the shame and disgrace of our nation, be it
said, her rulers yielded to their unmanly, ignoble
and cowardly request. To-day, like Samson, we
are shorn of our strength in one way, but we will

[Column Two]

abide the Lord’s time, when this nation will again
confer,unasked, the franchise upon the Women of
Utah.
	Oh, mothers! let us be alive to the mighty re-
sponsibilities of teaching and training our child-
ren in the principles of the everlasting Gospel.
Though we labor in obscurity and self-sacrifice,
that is only known by the angels, yet our labors
are not in vain, if we seek for wisdom and look to
the future good of those for whom we toil;
	"The kindly plans devised for other's good,
	So seldom guessed, so little understood,
	The quiet, steadfast love that sought to win
	Some wanderer from the woeful paths of sin,
					These are not lost."
	Our daily prayers for wisdom, patience and
firmness, our daily struggles for self-control and
discernment to avoid temptations, will all have an
influence for good. We cannot inspire our child-
ren with faith if we are lacking; we cannot teach
them Gospel truths if our own souls are not im-
bued with them; we cannot instill into their hearts
a high sense of duty to God and their fellow men,
if we are not guided by this important principle.
We may teach our little ones to pray, but if they
never see us bow in humble, earnest suplication,
what will it avail? We may plead with our
daughters to put away pride, while we toil in trim-
ming their clothing and bedecking ourselves in
many and fashionable changes of apparel. If we
were seeking first the kingdom of God and His
righteousness, would we not oftener lay aside our
oftimes vain and unfrofitable occupations, that
perish with the doing, and take our precious little
ones in our arms and impress upon their plastic
minds principles of truth that will fortify them
against the snares of the evil one? Oh, we think
we are too busy, oftimes with our fancy work, to
keep our little ones employed, while habits of
indolence are weaving destructive chains around
them. More anon.
						     SUN FLOWER.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


RECOLLECTIONS OF THE PAST.

	Who can realize how swiftly the tide or current
of time is carrying us onward to our eternal des-
tiny? Never before did I see this subject as
vividly as I did at the funeral of Sister Augusta
Cobb Young.
	It seemed but a few weeks since we met at Pitts-
burg. She was with a company of Saints from
Boston; we were with a company from Buffalo en
route for the same place. That place was
Nauvoo.
	It was in the year of 1843. Our captain was
Bradford W. Elliott; theirs, judging from appear-
ances, was President Brigham Young, at this
time President of the Twelve Apostles. It was
the first time we had seen any of the people of
Nauvoo. We were delighted to find ourselves in 
company with Heber C. Kimball, one of the
Twelve Apostles, Elders George A. Smith and
Daniel Spencer, the latter and his aged parents;
also father and mother Alley, to whom we gave
this title because of their age. They were going
to Nauvoo to see their daughter, who was the wife
of Bro. Bates Nobles.
	Sister Cobb at this time had a sick babe. We
stopped at Cincinnati three days; during this time
the babe died. Since that time all the above
named persons have died; only a step-sister and
myself survive. Sister Cobb died Feb. 2nd,
1887.
	There were others of the company whose fate I
did not learn, not being as familiarly acquainted
with them, excepting one family by the name of
Brown, at whose house we boarded while waiting
in Buffalo for the company to start. My step-sis-
ter, Ann Dunlap, and myself left Utica to join this
company, which would start in the fall. Our
parents had gone the previous season.

[column 2]
	When we arrived at Nauvoo we found that they
lived on a farm out of the city. I went to see Sis-
ter Brown, who had a sick child. I found her
comfortably situated, with food and fuel, etc., but
quite unhappy. “How do you feel,” she asked, “to
see the Twelve riding around in their carriages,
and we stuck down here in the mud?” “Well,” I
replied, “if they are out of the mud I am glad of
it; and if I am faithful I will see the day when I
can ride in my carriage.” I saw nothing to grum-
ble at if I could have health.
	She did not see the subject as I did, her child
got better, and the next time I inquired for them
I learned they had gone to St. Louis. They only
stayed in Nauvoo two weeks.
	I well remember how anxious I was to see Sister
Catherine Clawson, the mother of Bishop H. B.
Clawson. She was baptized in Utica at the time
that I was, and left the next spring for Nauvoo. I
went east about sixty miles to visit my mother’s
youngest sister. Mother could not think of going
so far west without bidding her goodbye.
	Aunt Mary had two sons and one daughter. This
daughter was very low with the consumption. She
said to me, “You must stay with mother, she will
be so lonely when I am gone.” She had been
raising blood for weeks, and was beyond the reach
of help. Mother consented to let me stay; and
Father Dunlap also consented to let Ann wait un-
til I was ready to go. She waited at Sister Mon-
roe’s in Utica, who had taught me the trade of
tailoring. My cousin, Ann Maria Dodge, only
lived two weeks, when she passed quietly away.
Peace be to her ashes, for she was a good girl.
She was twenty-two years of age, but knew noth-
ing of the Gospel. Mother received the Gospel
so joyfully I felt quite sure that Aunt Mary would,
as soon as she had time to investigate, but to my
astonishment she would not read anything, but
insisted that her minister would (figuratively
speaking) tear me all to pieces.
	“Well," said I, “then he must tear the Bible to
pieces, for I simply believe the Bible.” He came,
and in a very genteel manner tried to show me my
error. My arguments from the Scripture were
quite ahead of his, and I am sure he saw it. He,
beiieved in election, and accordingly all who are
elected to be saved would be. This ended the
argument.
	My uncle and aunt were not able to convince
me that our faith was wrong, and finally gave up
the idea of trying to do so. My sister came from
Utica, Oneida Co., to Glen, Montgomery Co., to
say that there would be a company start from
Buffalo in September. Accordingly we soon pre-
pared to start on our journey, and arrived in
Nauvoo, as above stated, in the month of October,
on the steamer Nautilus.
							M. E. KIMBALL.
	Salt Lake City, June 30, 1887.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

		BOX ELDER STAKE.
	The quarterly conference of the Relief So-
ciety of Box Elder Stake was held in the
Tabernacle at Brigham City, on the 14th of
June, 1887, at 10 o’clock a.m. After the usual
opening exercises, Coun. Olivia Widerborg ad-
dressed the assembly, and reported the Scandi-
navian Society favorably. Spoke of the great
mission of mothers in our home, in our actions,
and in every respect to set an example. 
	Prest. Harriet Snow said we expected Sister
Richards and Sister Shirtliff from Ogden, but 
perhaps they would be here before noon. “It
is our privilege to live up to the commandments
of the Lord; we are under a responsibility to
the Lord and each other.” Spoke of Sister
Keller, President of the Mantua Relief Society.
“She has been very sick for a long time; let us
all remember her in our prayers.”


Sister Dewey reported Deweyville verbally.
Sister Jensen reported Mantua Society, and
spoke feelingly about their dying President,
Sister Keller; told of her goodness, and the
comfort she had been to her sisters.
	Sister Anderson reported the 3rd Ward; Sis-
ter Peery, Three Mile Creek; Sister Albertsen,
Bear River City; Sister Watkins, the 4th Ward;
Sister Hubbard, Willard City; Sister Mary E.
Snow, 2nd Ward Brigham City; Sister Fannie
Graehl made excellent remarks. Sister Sus-
annah P. Booth reported the 1st Ward Brig-
ham City. All these sisters gave good instruc-
tions, and spoke upon the duties and responsi-
bilities of the mothers in Israel and the women
of Zion, touching upon many points of special
interest, and zealously urged all the sisters to
live humbly, and to be obedient to counsel and
prayerful at all times.
	Singing. Benediction by Prest. Harriet
Snow.

	Afternoon session: After singing and prayer,
minutes of the previous conference were read
and accepted.
	Prest. Harriet Snow said, "There are many
duties resting upon us, as members of this
Church and members and officers of the Relief
Society. It is necessary that we should culti-
vate a feeling of love and union. If we em-
brace these opportunities of meeting together,
gathering intelligence and wisdom, when we 
get thoroughly schooled in the principles of
our religion, then we shall be strong and firm,
without fears, but if we are negligent we shall 
lose the spirit of our religion.
	Emilia D. Madsen spoke a short time on
truthfulness and the way of gaining each other's 
confidence.
	Prest. Jane S. Richards, of Weber Stake
R. S., said, "I was very pleased to hear the 
minutes, and Sister Snow and Madsen. I feel
there is a good spirit here." Spoke of the great
difficulty in keeping a secret. "Our religion is 
of that nature that we can find plenty to do; I
never knew a time when there was more oppor-
tunity to do good than at present. I would
not care for religion if it was only for to-day
or to-morrow, but it is for all eternity if we
live right. When we meet together in the 
Spirit of the Lord, talking of His mercy and
love, don't we feel good—much better than
if we go to all the festivals?" Spoke of the 
many duties of women. "The men could not
get along without us; we could not get along 
without them. If anything goes wrong with
me, that I don't feel good when I go to bed, I
have to get rid of it before I lay down to 
sleep. I want to feel happy and enjoy life
while here." Knew how hard it was for
mothers of a family to come to meeting, and 
how much patience they had to exercise. "I
desire to live faithful. I think I can feel that
you are going onward in Brigham City. I 
used to think if I could only keep faithful till
I was forty years old I would be pretty sure,
but I have seen people seventy years old aposta-
tize; it is only those who keep faithful to the 
end who will receive the reward."
	Sister Shirtliff said, "I feel here a spirit of
sisterly love  Woman's work is a very mixed 
one. I think there is a great field of charity
for women to do good for each other; we can
find much to do among the sick and the poor;
wherever we can assist each other we should do
so happily; if it is not put down in our minute
book it will be recorded in heaven."
	Sister Harding bore her testimony. Spoke
of the great danger in letting our girls go out
to the Gentiles.
	Bro. Dudley said, "I have enjoyed what I 
have heard. It would do me good if I could
put it into practice. The Spirit of God has
been with us to-day."
	The congregation sang, "We thank Thee,
[Column Two]
O God, for a prophet." Adjourned for three
months.
				EMILIA D. MADSEN, Sec.

			SALT LAKE STAKE
	The Relief Society quarterly conference of
this Stake was held in the 14th Ward Assembly
Rooms, Thursday, June 23, 1887, at 10 a.m.,
Mrs. M. Isabella Horne presiding. On the stand
were Mrs. Z. D. H. Young, Mrs. B. W. Smith 
and Mrs. H. M. Whitney. Meeting opened
with the usual exercises and verbal reports of 
the presidents. The reports showed that the
several organizations were in good condition,
and an increased attendance, and an improve-
ment in good works.
	After the reports were given, Prest. Horne
addressed the sisters on the great importance
of the duties assigned them in the Relief So-
ciety, and urged them to a diligent performance
of the same.
	Meeting was adjourned until 2 p.m. with 
singing, and prayer was offered by Sister B.
W. Smith.

	Afternoon session: There were many more
present, and the presidents from country
places gave reports of the condition of the
society over which they presided, all being in 
prosperity and in improvement in good works.
	Sister M. A. Hyde, President of the Relief
Society in Sanpete Stake, then addressed the
meeting on the cultivation and education of
the children of Zion; also spoke upon the
principle of plural marriage, considering it the
highest type of marriage, and spoke of the
very fine, noble children growing up in our
midst; thought we should bring them up intelli-
gently, and gave much excellent instruction.
	Prest. Horne spoke upon the storing of grain,
referred to the fall of Babylon, and the prep-
aration necessary for that great event; also
gave counsel about supporting by membership
and donations the Deseret Hospital.
	“Sister Zina” spoke briefly upon the times in
which we are living, the great events transpir-
ing, and the crisis in this kingdom; thought
our work would enlarge, etc.
	Coun. H. M. Whitney thought this work
was rolling on with mighty speed; referred to
her father's sons who were zealous in the work, 
and made allusion to the principle of plural
marriage as a divine order, etc.
	Sister M. A. Freeze, Prest. of the Y. L. of 
this Stake, made some very appropriate re-
marks, and also spoke of plural marriage as
from God, and thought it a subject women
should speak upon, etc.
	Sister Hyde spoke in the gift of tongues, and 
Sister Young gave the interpretation, after
which Prest. Horne made closing remarks, and
the meeting was adjourned for three months 
with singing and a benediction by Sister Sarah
M. Kimball.
			Y. L. M. I. A.
	There was an interesting quarterly confer-
ence of the Y. L. held on Friday, June 24th,
in the 14th Ward Hall, though there were not 
so many as usual present.  Mrs. M. A. Freeze
presided, assisted by her Counselor, Mrs. M. Y.
Dougall.  Verbal reports were given by the
officers of the several Associations in the Stake,
though all were not reported, which is a matter
of regret, as at these conferences general in-
struction is given for the benefit of all and
their future progress, and it is expected that
each Association will be heard from. These 
young women in their organizations are mak-
ing rapid improvement in such studies as are
calculated to develop their mental and spiritual 
powers, and give them a thorough knowledge
of their religion, their own and other countries,
and the science of government. They also
contribute to charitable purposes of various
kinds, and each association pays, or is expected
[Column Three]
to pay, monthly to the Deseret Hospital, re-
ceiving credit for the full amount, which may
be refunded in payment on any patient they 
choose to send to the institution for treatment. 
A short time ago Prest. Freeze proposed that 
the Associations in this Stake assist in emigrat-
ing some worthy persons from the old country
who were not in circumstances to pay their own 
emigration.  Means has been already collected
sufficient to bring out three adult persons, and
this plainly shows that "where there's a will
there's a way," and furthermore, that the Lord
blesses those who are willingly helpful to the
poor. Sweet charity! well doth it become the
young, and in  blessing are we blest, and in
giving more than in receiving.
	The meeting was addressed by Sisters R. R.
Grant, E. Howard, B. W. Smith and M. A.
Hyde, women of large experience in the
Church, whose teachings to the young are ever
motherly and wise.
	Dr. Ellis R and Maggie C. Shipp each spoke
to the young ladies on appropriate subjects, in
a most interesting manner, calculated to awaken
thought and inspire noble sentiments. They
were also addressed by some of the leading 
officers of these valuable Associations for the
advancement of the young daughters of Zion.
Some matters of business were transacted, and 
the conference adjourued for three months.
			ONEIDA STAKE.
	A Relief Society conference of the Oneida
Stake of Zion, held in Clifton District, June
8th, 1887. Meeting opened at 10 o'clock a.m.
Present on the stand, Stake President, Eliza-
beth Fox, Second Counselor, Mary Hawks,
President Hendricks of Oxford, President Jane
M. Howell and her two Counselors of Clifton,
and Counselor Mary Ann Paskin of Treasure-
ton; also Patriarch Milo Andrus of Oxford,
Elders William Webster and Lewis Petty.
The choir sang, "An angel from on high."
Prayer was offered by Elder William Webster.
The choir sang, "Truth reflects upon our
senses." President Fox invited all the presi-
dents and representatives present to come to the
stand. Minutes of the previous conference
read and received.
	Prest. J. M. Howell represented the Clifton
ton Society as being fully organized and in 
good order. 
	Prest. Hendricks reported the Oxford Society
as doing well.  Said the sisters had done con-
siderable work since she had presided, and felt
well and benefitted by the sisters' visit.
	Coun Mary Ann Paskin reported the
Treasureton branch as doing well.
	Coun. Mary Hawks said, "I am happy to
meet you once more, dear sisters. We have 
traveled many miles in company with the
brethren of this Stake, and have met many of 
the sisters and held quite a number of meetings. 
I am pleased to hear that the brethren meet 
with you once in a while; it is very encourag-
ing to the sisters. We should be kind to each 
other, and especially to those who are left to
provide for their families without the aid of
father and husband; they should not be neg-
lected. We should be very wise in counsel-
ing our children, for they are our hope and our
all. There is a great labor for us to perform
as sisters. You should attend your meetings,
and especially the Relief Society meetings,
where you can talk upon subjects that cannot 
by talked on in other meetings. Mothers, I 
say to you all, read good books, and often lecture
on the laws of human nature. You should
guard against all things which are injurious to 
your health. Do not neglect your duties in the
society for your work; if you wait for the time
when you have nothing to do you will never go,
for a mother's work is never done. Exercise
faith in the ordinances of the Gospel. We, as 
mothers, have a right to administer to the sick
in the absence of the priesthood.'


Prest. Fox said, “I am well pleased with the
reports and minutes of Relief Society in this
Stake. I feel to encourage in lecturing, and I
will advise the young ladies to meet with you
and receive the benefit. The teachers should
visit regularly. You should be diligent in
looking after the poor in your wards. Cheer
up the sisters, especially the ones who are left
alone. We are a blest people, for we live in
the age of building temples and working in
them for those who have died without a kuowl-
edge of the Gospel. Girls should not go into
the creeks to bathe, but they should bathe often
in proper places in warm water. I will say to
the girls, be virtuous and chaste, and never
dare to neglect to pray. I say to the sisters,
attend to your meetings, so you may be bene-
fited and your officers be encouraged. Do not
neglect to lay up grain and take care of it. I
would like the brethren to help build good
granaries, so the mice cannot destroy the grain;
our grain is very precious, and should be taken
good care of.”
	Elder William Webster said, “The Sisters
are traveling in the Stake to instruct the sisters
and children upon many subjects which are
necessary for them to understand. We have
repented, been baptized, and many of us have
received forgiveness of sins, and are in posses-
sion of a portion of the Holy Ghost, whose
office is to lead to all truth. We should com-
ply with all the requirements of the Gospel so
we may be counted with the worthy at the last
when Christ shall come to make up His 
jewels.”
	Patriarch Milo Andrus of Oxford said, “I
have felt inspired in your meeting; I have felt
inspired by the beautiful morning, and by the
appearance of life and growth outside of the
house. It is very important for us all to under-
stand the laws of life and health. The Gospel
found us all in a very ignorant state pertaining
to the knowledge of God. The brethren should
sustain the Relief Society by their presence.
Joseph the Prophet, and Hyrum the Patriarch,
and many more noble brethren, set the example
in their life-time. The Society should try to
create work for their daughters and sisters, that
they may not be compelled to go to the outside
for employment. The young people should
not dare to trifle with the springs of life, that
their offspring may be noble sons and daughters
of God, as He has reserved the most noble
spirits to come forth in this age. By observing
the laws of God all this can be brought about.
We should so live that we can dedicate our
bodies to God, feeling we have done His will
and kept His commandments. There is no
danger, truth will prevail. This kingdom will
come off victorious; only wait.”
	Bishop William Garner said, “I am thank-
ful for the visit of the sisters, and for their
timely instruction.”
	Prest. Fox thanked those present for their
good attention.
	Conference was then adjourned subject to ap-
pointment. The choir sang, ”O, say, what is
truth?” Benediction by Elder Lewis Petty.
	While the Relief Society held their meeting
in the ward house, the Stake Presidency of the
Primary held a meeting in the Society Hall,
which was crowded to overflowing.
	At 2 o’clock a.m. a ward meeting was held,
Stake Counselor George C. Parkinson presiding.
The time was occupied first by Elder William
Webster of Franklin, secondly, by Elder Riley
Davis, and lastly, by Coun. George C. Parkin-
son. All gave wise and timely instructions.
Conn. Saul Hale and wife, both of the Stake
Presidency, were prevented from attending this
conference by an accident.
	The enjoyable visit of our honored brethren
and sisters, with so much good instruction, was

[column 2]
crowded is so short a time, that this report
hardly does credit to the occasion.
					D. E. DUDLEY, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

	It is with deep feelings of regret and sorrow that we re-
cord the death of one of our most esteemed sisters, Sophia
Berg, who departed this life Dec. 10th, 1886, at her home
in Santaquin, Utah, after a long and painful sickness, caused
by consumption. Deceased was born in Var a Finnland,
May 19th, 1845. She was a faithful teacher in the Relief
Society, meek and humble in spirit and exemplary in all
the relations of life. She was beloved by all who knew her,
and died in the hope of a glorious resurrection, to inherit
eternal life in the mansions of the just. She leaves a hus-
band and aged mother, with a large circle of relatives and
friends to mourn her loss.

	DIED, at her home in Santaquin, Sister Annie Parson
Swenson, May 16th, 1887, from a lingering illness of about
nine months duration. Her age was 66 years, 8 months
and 14 days. She was bereft of her husband in a very
sudden and cruel way some fifteen years since, and has
lived a widow ever since. She labored hard supporting
herself and donating as liberally as her circumstances would
permit to aid in the great work she was engaged in. She
was a very faithful and efficient teacher in the Relief Society,
which office she has held with honor for the last seventeen
years, and was always ready to faithfully perform any duty
that was required of her. She embraced the Gospel in her
native country, Swedon, in the year 1857; and emigrated
to Utah in the year 1860. She leaves an adopted daughter
and a large circle of friends to mourn her loss.

	To the memory of Sister Edna F. Tietjen, the beloved
wife of August Tietjen, who died June 15th. 1887. She
was born in Mecklenberg, Strilts, Germany, Sept. 8th,
1825, and emigrated to Utah with her husband and family
in the year 1859, having previously united themselves with
the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. From
that time forward she was devoted to her religion. Her
door was always open to receive and hospitably entertain
the servants of God, and to all who sought her for aid and
counsel. She held the office of treasurer in the Relief So-
ciety here for the past sixteen year reflecting great credi
on herself and honor to the Association. She was modes
and retiring, yet possessing many rare qualities of heart and
mind. She died as she had lived, beloved and respected
by all who knew her, leaving a husband, eight children and
twenty-six grandchildren, also a large circle of friends to
mourn her loss.

	These three dear sisters now at rest
		Were highly prized by those who knew their worth.
	Their missions were to comfort and to bless,
		While God saw fit to lend them on the earth;
	They labored with untiring zeal.
		Nor deemed their tasks too hard to bear,
	Till God in mercy called them home— 
		Bright crowns and homes await them there.

	Resolutions of Respect on the death of the above
named sisters adopted at a meeting of the Relief Society
of Santaquin, held July 7th, 1887.
	Whereas, Their labors in our Association have been
faithfully performed, we feel that some proper recognition
of their virtues be recognized among us, therefore, be it
	Resolved, That we, as members of the Relief Society, do
deeply mourn the loss of such valuable and faithful co-
laborers.
	Resolved, That we present a copy of these Resolutions
to their relatives, and have the same published in the Ex-
ponext; and keep a copy on our record.

					ELIZABETH STICKNEY, Prest.
					ELIZABETH WALL,
		Committee.	BETTIE BOSS,
					HANNAH KIRKMAN,
					ANN J, STICKNEY, Sec.,


Dr. Romania B. Pratt, 
Office and Residence at the Deseret Hospital,
		Seventeenth Ward, Salt Lake City.

The Warm Springs car passes the Hospital, which 
can also be reached by Telephone No. 269.
