SALT LAKE CITY,	-	OCTOBER 15, 1887.


OCTOBER.

October is the month that seems
All woven with midsummer dreams;
She brings for us the golden days
That fill the air with smoky haze;
She brings for us the lisping breeze,
And wakes the gossips in the trees,
Who whisper in the vacant nest
Forsaken	by its feathered guest.
Now half the birds forget to sing,
And half of them have taken wing,
Before their pathway shall be lost
Beneath the gossamer of frost;
Now one by one the gay leaves fly
Zigzag across the yellow sky;
They rustle here and flutter there,
Until the bough hangs chill and bare.
What joy for us—what happiness
Shall cheer the day, the night shall bless?
’Tis Hallow-e’en, the very last
Shall keep for us remembrance fast.
When every child shall duck the head
To find the precious pippin red!
—Frank Dempster Shermam in St. Nicholas for October.


OCTOBER CONFERENCE.
	The semi-annual conference held this month in
Salt Lake City, was replete with interest through-
out. The first morning the Tabernacle was
crowded, and each day thereafter, morning and
afternoon; and on Sunday hundreds went away
unable to obtain seats, while many stood in the
aisles and doorways during the service. A great
deal of emotion was evinced when President
Wilford Woodruff came on the stand just previous
to the commencement, of the afternoon services.
Never upon any Sabbath day in the Tabernacle
was there such an expression made of es-
teem and reverence as when this aged man
and servant of God appeared in the midst of
the people, after an absence of nearly three
years. It must have been a testimony to
him that he could not fail to appreci-
ate of the genuine love and esteem of the Latter-
day Saints. God grant him many such oppor-
tunities of meeting with the Saints, and may he
live to accomplish all that he desires to do for the
living and the dead, and his enemies have no
power to hinder him in the performance of the
great work to which he has been called.
	It is something to rejoice in, under the circum-
stances. that at such a multitudinous gathering as
that of Sunday, the 9th of October, that so many
of the quorum of the Apostles could be present,
and that the people might enjoy the privilege of
looking upon their faces, and of hearing them
speak and bear testimony. It was a good day for
Israel, and no doubt many hundreds went away
not only edified and instructed and comforted, but
happier in spirit and feeling at having been per-
mitted to behold again those who have borne in a
great measure the responsibility of the establish-
ing of this kingdom, and from time to time perse-
cution, affliction, hardship and privation, yet re-


mained firm, steadfast and immoveable in those
eternal principles upon which the Gospel is
founded.
	And thus, while passing through difficulties and
suffering for the truth’s sake, the Saints have
reason to rejoice in it all, knowing that the triumph
is sure, and the reward cannot fail.


[Column 1]
		﻿THE MINORITY REPORT.

	It is a little satisfaction to know that there are,
among the Utah Commission, two men whose
opinions on the subject of more legislation for Utah
differ essentially from the majority, and who seem
to have some conscientious convictions, and are
not in favor of more severe laws or enforcement
of laws than what are already in force. The mi-
nority report to which we refer contains some
quotations from Madison and Jefferson that bear
strongly upon the question treated upon and every
true lover of justice would do well to bear them in
mind. Reference is also made to the Supreme
Court of the United States and the quotation made
is in unmistakable language.” The Supreme
Court of the United States has declared that
Laws are made for the government of actions
and while they cannot interfere with mere religious
belief, they may with practices. Con-
gress cannot pass a law for the government of the
Territories which shall prohibit the free exercise
of religion. The first amendment to the constitu-
tion expressly forbids such legislation. Religious
freedom is guaranteed everywhere throughout the
United States so far as Congressional interference
is concerned. [8 Otto, 145.]
	Madison says sententiously: “Religion, or the
duty we owe to the Creator, is not within the pro-
vince of civil government.”
	Jefferson says: “Believing that religion is a mat-
ter which lies solely between man and his God;
and that he owes account to none other for his
faith or his worship; that the legislative
powers of the government reach actions only and
not opinions, I contemplate with solemn reverence
that act of the whole American people which de-
clares that their legislature should make no law
respecting an establishment of religion, or pro-
hibiting the free exercise thereof." [8 Jefferson's
Work, 113]
	Upon the passage of the Act of March, 3, 1887,
similar views were expressed by distinguished
senators from Vermont and Kansas.
	Acting upon these fundamental canons, and in
accordance with the acts of Congress,
the commission has from time to time,
in official reports and otherwise, assured the Mor-
mon people that the government of the United 
States had no design to coerce them concerning
their church membership or their religious opin-
ions; and that all that was required; and all that
could rightfully be required, was that they
should come within the laws and abandon the
practice of polygamy. For example, in the com-
mission's first annual report, of November 17, 
1882, it said: "the legislation of Congress as we
understand it is not enacted against the religion of
any portion of the people of this territory. The
law under which we are acting is directed against
the crime of polygamy."
	These gentlemen give their ideas concerning plu-
ral marriage and its effects, which are entirely dis-
proved by facts as they exist here, but as it is not
expected that those outside the principles of the
faith of the Church can comprehend this higher
order of marriage as the Latter-day Saints do, it
is not strange that they should agree with other
writers upon the subject. Still men spending so
much time in the midst of this people, if they
were freefrom prejudice and disposed to investigate
carefully would be likely to arrive at different con-
clusions than such as they express in the following
paragraph:
	"In a larger view, polygamy is adjudged by the

[Column 2]
most enlightened nations to be a manifold
evil. It is the parent of caprice, cruelty and
license. It enervates the male and degrades the
female. Socially, politically and physically, it is
corrupting and deteriorating. Despotic in the
family, it is the prototype of despotism in the gov-
ernment. It largely accounts for the differing
characteristic of the Asiatic and European; for
the indolence and feebleness of the one, and the
energy and enterprise of the other. Inferiority is
its badge. In the armed contests of rival civiliza-
tions alike in ancient Greece and modern India,
it succumbed to the superiority of monogamy. It
is at variance with the divine economy if that,
originally God created but one man and one
woman, Adam and Eve, each as the only partner
in wedlock of the other. Logically and as a con-
sequence, it is irreconcilable to the idea of the
marriage covenant as practiced and revered by the
masterful Teuton, Celt and Anglo Saxon.
	In the matter of abolishing "polygamy" by en-
forcing at all hazards the Edmunds-Tucker bill
with all the pains and penalties thereunto apper-
taining, Congress nor the officers of the law have
never considered the cruelties thus in-
flicted upon women and children (leav-
ing men out of the question.) They
do not consider that the plural wife loves
her husband as sincerely and devotedly as the first
wife—that the children are completely robbed of
the parental protection of the father, as far as it is
in the power of Congress to do so. Can it be
possible that these men think seriously of the
broken hearts and ruined homes, and of the
many who have died in consequence? Or do
they deem it so small a matter to enter the sacred
precincts of a loving home, and rend asunder the
most holy ties of husband and wife, who have
solemnly covenanted before God and angels, not
only for time, but for all eternity? There is, how-
ever, a boundary over which they cannot pass, and
where they can have no jurisdiction, nor can they
sit in judgment there on their fellow beings.
	The questions propounded to Judge Zane by 
Hon. John A. McClernand of the Utah Commis-
sion, who, with A. B. Carlton made the Minority
Report to the Secretary of the Interior at Wash-
ington, D. C., are to the point, and the answers
are positive and brief:
	1st. Whether, in your opinion, the existing
laws, diligently and strictly enforced, may be
reasonably relied on to work the cessation of
polygamy as a practice?
	2d. Whether any case originating in the com-
mission of the crime of polygamy since the date
of the Edmunds-Tucker act has come under you
judicial notice?
	3d. Whether, in your opinion, the alternative
provisions of that act extending the electoral
franchise to those complying with their conditions,
and denying it to those not complying with them,
or who are otherwise disqualified, have materially
prompted the present movement for a constitu-
tional inhibition of polygamy.
(Signed.) 	Your Obedient Servant,
				JOHN A. MCCLERNAND.
	To the first question propounded within, I
answer yes.
	To the second question, I answer no.
	To the third question, I answer yes.
	(Signed.)					C. S. ZANE.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
	OUR readers will readily understand that as we
published the Epistle of President Woodruff and
Council, that there is little room for Relief Society
Reports or other general matter. The Juab Stake
minutes of R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. and P. A., and
also Box Elder, Summit, and Tooele Stakes are
on hand, and will have place in the coming
issues of the paper.


THE report of the Utah Commission has been
publised in full in the Deseret News and Salt Lake
Herald, and we think every woman
as well as every man should read it and see for
themselves just how the "Mormon" question is
viewed by the Commission and how needful they
think it to have further legislation. In consider-
ing this subject it seems impossible for men with
human hearts to ask for further persecution of a
people already, as it were, pushed to the extreme;
how much more the Commission think can possi-
bly be borne one cannot even imagine.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

		SAN JUAN STAKE. 

	The conference of the Relief Society and
Y. L. M. I. A. of San Juan Stake was held at
Mancos, Colorado, Aug. 27th, 1887, Prest.
J. M. Walton presiding. After the usual open-
ing exercises, the different wards were reported,
all of which were in good condition. Sister
Martha Hammond was chosen Second Counselor
to J. M. Walton, and was unanimously sus-
tained, and was set apart by President F. A.
Hammond. All the sisters manifested a good
spirit, and desired to live their religion. 
	The Young Ladies occupied a portion of the
time, Prest. Moselle Hall presiding. The
Young Ladies’ Associations of the different
wards were reported in good condition, and all
feeling well. Singing. Benediction by Bishop
Jens Nielson.
			JANE M. WALTON, Prest.,
			JULIA BUTT, Sec.
			
			JUAREZ.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Thinking you would like to hear something
from our Relief Society in far off Mexico, I
will give you a brief account of our organiza-
tion.
	Our Relief Society was organized July 7th,
1887, Sister Elizabeth Hawkins, Presi-
dent; Sarah A. Corden and Phebe Fife, Coun-
selors; Elizabeth Haines Secretary, and Salena
Corden, Treasurer Apostle Erastus Snow was
present and assisted in organizing, also Sister
Minerva W. Snow was with us, and gave many
good instructions. We hold our meetings
semi-monthly. We have thirty-nine names
enrolled, and fifteen average attendance. Most
of the people enjoy good health, and appreci-
ate the peace we enjoy here.
			ELIZABETH HAWKINS, Prest.
			ELIZABETH HAINES, Sec.
			
			MARICOPA STAKE.
	The Relief Society of Maricopa Stake held
their quarterly conference at Zenos, Sept. 22nd.
1887,commencing at 1 p.m., Mrs.E. G. McDon-
ald presiding. A number of the brethren were
present, including Bro. Henry Rogers and
Charles I. Robson of the Stake Presidency;
also Bishop Pomeroy and Counselors. After
the usual preliminaries of singing and prayer,
the roll of Stake officers was called and minutes
of previous conference read and approved.
	Prest. E. G. McDonald made a few remarks,
and prayed that the Spirit of God would direct
us in our meeting. Desired to hear the reports
from the different branches. This was re-			
sponded to with the exception of the Tempe
branch, which was not in working order, on
account of the general move being made from
that branch.
	Coun. Adelaide Allen represented the Mesa
Ward; Prest. Lizzie McDonald, Lehi; Prest.
Ann Kleinman, Almy. All spoke in praise of
the faithful few in their several branches; the
few who were always ready with generous
hearts and willing hands to comfort those who
were in trouble and assist the needy; always
[Column 2]									
present at their meetings, and faithful to their
duties connected, with their organization.
	Prest. E. G. McDonald felt gratified with
the good reports which had been given, but felt
there was an inexcusable slackness with a good
many in regard to their meetings. Had visited
the different branches since last conference,and
felt gratified with the good spirit which at-
tended their meetings. Spoke upon the sub-
ject of slander and of the baneful influence it
exercised in a community if given way to; it
should not have a place in our midst; it had
brought sorrow and heart-aches to many an 
innocent person. Spoke of the trying times 
the Saints were passing through; let them re-
joice and trust in God, for He alone can carry
us safely through. Wished to hear from the
brethren present.
	Bro. Henry Rogers was pleased to respond
to the call made upon him. Spoke in regard
to the first organization of the Relief Society
by Joseph Smith, the Prophet of the Most
High God. They were intended for good, and
were a great benefit when carried on properly.
“Let us avoid the spirit of fault-finding of the
ward, stake, or society.” Compared the
Saints to wheat and tares. “Are we wheat or
tares?” Exhorted the Saints to be faithful to
all their duties, that they might retain a name
and place in the Church and kingdom of God.
	Bro. Charles Robson spoke of the evil influ-
ences which are around us continually to lead
us astray. “Good and evil are placed before
us, which will we choose? Let us secure
eternal life by choosing the good continually.”
Spoke of the benefit of prayer. “Our hearts
should be lifted in prayer to God while we are
at our labors, and blessings will flow on every
hand. Let us pray for our officers, instead of
criticising them.” Gave some good instruc-
tions in regard to the principles of obedience;
exhorted mothers to teach this principle to their
children.	
	They were followed in their remarks by
Bishops Pomeroy and Stewart, who felt to en-
dorse what had been said, and to encourage the
sisters in their good work.
	The names of the Relief Society officers of
the Stake were then presented to the conference,
and were sustained unanimously; the statistical
and financial report was also read by the Secre-
tary.
	After a few remarks by the President, con-
ference adjourned for three months. Singing,
“Guide us, O, Thou Great Jehovah.” Bene-
diction by Bro. Charles Allen.
			    SARAH M. POMEROY, Sec.


﻿IDLENESS.

DEAR SISTERS:
	The inspired servants of God, both ancient and
modern, prove that idleness is a sin. Idleness
is not commendable in any one. Industry and
diligence in any useful pursuit is a becoming
trait in any one’s character. The Lord, in one
of His revelations to Joseph Smith, gives this
expressive injunction to the Saints:
	“Cease to be idle; cease to be unclean; cease
to find fault with one another; retire to thy bed
early, that ye may not be weary; arise early,
that your bodies and your minds may be in-
vigorated."
	This counsel is not for the poor alone, but
for all who would be accepted of the Lord.
No person is justified, whatever may be his
fortune, in spending his time in idleness. Not
only is industry enjoined by the Almighty,
but it is happiness in this life, which we are
all striving for. The person who thinks that
happiness is to be found in luxurious idleness
makes a great mistake. Industry brings health
and the ability to confer benefits upon others.
The person who has never felt the joy and


satisfaction that results from this cause, has
never known what genuine happiness is.
	Industry is honorable, idleness is degrading.
Girls, in making choice of a husband, shun the
idler, or the person who wishes to make a liv-
ing without work. If there are any of our ac-
quaintances who think there are better ways of
making a living than by honest labor, they are
mistaken, and beware of allowing your affec-
tions to go out toward them in the least. If
you join fortunes with such a person for life,
the chances are you will have to support him,
or be degraded and mortified by seeing him
resort to dishonest methods of securing a liv-
ing. Think of the words of the wise man,
“Seest thou a man diligent in business.”
					HATTIE ADAMS.
	Nephi.


﻿DON’T, MOTHERS!

	An indignant but sensible friend of mine 
exclaimed the other day, “There should be a
law passed against tiny infants being paraded 
in public.” If mothers with babies three or 
four weeks old are able, with perfect safety for
their future as well as present good to be on
the streets, (which I very much doubt in this
degenerate age) well and good, if you run out
for an hour or two for a “change” but oh, do 
leave the little red, wriggling, writhing, bits
of humanity safe at home in their cosy cribs
away from the glare of sun and changes of
wind and weather.
	The public are not particularly interested in
your individual wonder, and there is such a
feeling of pity expressed for the little martyrs
on exhibition in trailing and multitudious
skirts, elaborate cloaks and fluffy hoods. Do 
let the downy human birdlings stay in the 
home nest until full fledged.		         	L.


GEMS.

	“The seeds of love can never grow but under
the warm and genial influence of kindly feel-
ings and affectionate manners.”

	“I believe in the democracy of the family.
If in this world there is anything splendid, it
is a home where all are equal.”

	The path from an open violation of con
science to a condition of religious doubt, is as
direct as that which leads to heaven.—J. G.
Holland.

	“In life it is difficult to say who do us the
most injury and work the worst mischief, ene-
mies with the worst intentions, or friends with
the best.”

	“An exacting temper is one against which to
guard both one’s own heart and the nature of
those who are under our control and influence.
To give and to allow, to suffer and to bear are
graces more to the purpose of a noble life than
c[-]ld, exacting selfishness, which must have, let
who will go without; which will not yield, let
who will break. It is a disastrous quality
wherewith to go through the world; for it re-
c[-]ives as much pain as it inflicts, and creates
the discomfort it deprecates.”


Princess Louise is said not to be the timid
dainty dame that princesses of the blood are
said to be. She has steered a canoe through
some of the roughest shallows of the Resti-
gouche, and has climbed perilous peaks at
dawn to make sunrise sketches. While in
Canada, she frequently returned from a ride or
drive at an unexpected hour, and, not finding
the groom at his post, rolled up her sleeves and
groomed the pony herself.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


TO A FRIEND.

	The world can never give thee to my arms,
	Nor from my heart thy image tear;
	It rests secure 'mid life's alarms,
	A shrine, and sacred image there.
	No human sounding lines can tell
	The secrets of that sunless well,
	Across the crowd our glances meet,
	With laughing lip, no long drawn sighs,
	But in our inmost souls we read,
	"The sad embrace of pining eyes,"
	Naming us kindred, fond and dear,
	In some past time, in some lost sphere.
					ELIZABETH LISLE SAXON.


GOOD-BYE.

Good-bye, good-bye, it is the sweetest blessing
	That falls from mortal lips on mortal ear,
The weakness of our human love confessing,
	The promise that a love more strong is near—
		May God be with you !

Why do we say it when the tears are starting?
	Why must a word so sweet bring only pain?
Our love seems all-sufficient till the parting,
	And then we feel it impotent and vain—
		May God be with you !

Oh, may He guide, and bless, and keep you ever,
	He who is strong to battle with your foes;
Whoever fails, His love can fail you never,
	And all you need He in His wisdom knows—
		May God be with you !

Better than earthly presence, e'en the dearest,
	Is the great blessing that our partings bring;
For in the loneliest moments God is nearest,
	And from our sorrows heavenly comforts spring,
		If God be with us.

Good-bye, good.bye, with latest breath we say it,
	A legacy of hope, and faith, and love;
Parting must come, we cannot long delay it,
	But, one in Him, we hope to meet above,
		If God be with us.

Good-bye—'tis all we have for oue another;
	Our love, more strong than death, is helpless still,
For none can take the burden from h[-]s brother,
	Or shield, except by prayer, from any ill—
		May God be with you!
								—Selected.


[Column 1]
SUGGESTIONS.

	In the word of wisdom, revealed to the
Prophet Joseph Smith, Feb. 27th, 1833, there
is a deep mine of wealth as yet only partially
explored, even by the Latter-day Saints, for
whose especial good it was given. Many beau-
tiful truths yet remain to be discoursed by
those who meditate upon this very comprehen-
sive revelation and seek for the same spirit by
which it was given, for truly "none can com-
prehend the things of God, but by the Spirit of
God."
	In that day the Lord said this revelation
was "to be sent greeting, not by commandment,
or constraint, but by the word of wisdom, 
showing forth the order and will of God in the
temporal salvation of all Saints in the last
days. Given for a principle, with a promise
adapted to the capacity of the weak and the
weakest of all Saints, who are or can be called
Saints; but at the general conference held Oct.,
1885, Apostle H. J. Grant, one of the mouth
pieces of the Lord, to whom He has promised
to give line upon line, and precept upon pre-
cept for the benefit of His children, as they
may require from day to day, said, "The word
of wisdom is adapted to all, and it comes to us
now by commandment, and is therefore obliga-
tory upon all to practice the same in the spirit
in which it was given."
	God designed man to live long upon the
earth in health and strength, with power to
labor and accomplish all He required of him;

[Column 2]
He designed him to live to the age of a tree,
not dragging out a miserable existence, but full
of life and vitality; but such a state is impos-
sible according to the systems of living adopted
by man, which tend to degenerate, but the word
of wisdom is full of the principles of regenera-
tion, and if observed, will gradually bring us
back to our normal condition as enjoyed by
the ancients, who could number their days by
the hundreds, not being cut off untimely by
disease, but yielding to old age because death
was pronounced upon them within a thousand
years, or a day of the Lord's time. 
	After defining the various uses to which the
products of the earth are to be put for the bene-
fit of man, the Lord expressly forbids the use
of "strong drink," but says, "It is for the
WASHING OF YOUR BODIES" Now, I think
the full import of this sentence is better under-
stood by the Saints in general as it was by  my-
self a few years ago. Though I had read the
revelation from childhood, and had avoided
the use of tea, coffee, and, to a certain extent,
other forbidden articles, still for many years I
was in a low, debilitated state of health, a
victim to a species of muscular rheumatism
of the spine, and other complications, which 
made life a weary burden, which, but for a
mighty faith in God, and an irrepressible hope,
I would have gladly yielded up as a cross too
heavy to bear. Being impressed with the
necessity of cleanliness, I strictly observed its
rules, as far as possible, though the bath was
my constant terror, in consequence of the ex-
cessive pain, fatigue and languor it occasioned,
also for the painful stiffness which invariably
followed, though using, as I thought, the ut-
most caution. One of our lady physicians,
who firmly believes that science and true religion
always agree, advised the free use of alcohol
over the entire surface of the body after each
ablution, referring me to this item contained
in the word of wisdom. I took the advice
of the Great Physician, revealed anew to me
individually—through His daughter, herself a
disciple of the divine art of healing, and soon
discerned that the bath, instead of being a
means of torture, became a source of pleasure
as well as profit. From that time I gradually
regained lost power, and feel that I have found
at least one of the hidden treasures, and am 
beginning to realize the promise, "That all
Saints who remember to keep and do these 
sayings, walking in obedience to the command-
ments, shall run and not be weary, shall walk 
and not faint."
	I have also learned through experience that
children suffering from any form of lung com-
plaint, or the weakness and debility attending
summer complaints, are greatly benefitted by 
being well rubbed with liquor. More than fifty
years ago the Lord again revealed—the first
time as revealed in the Apostle James, 5th
chapter, 14th verse, and again through His
Latter-day Prophet—the virtue of anointing
the sick with oil. This principle was not ac-
cepted at that time in the medical world, but
has since become universally acknowledged of
the utmost value in all fevers, though as yet
but few believe that "the effectual fervent
prayer of a righteous man availeth much."
The world may reject the fact of an open
channel of communication between the Creator
and His children, and particularly concerning
temporal matters, but this can be nothing less
than an insult to the Fountain of all intelli-
gence, who is the same yesterday, to-day and
forever. His power has not diminished, but
the lack of faith compelled Him to withdraw
His revelations from the children of men, but
He has again spoken from the heavens, He is
still the giver of every good and perfect gift,
and the day is near at hand when every knee
must bow and every tongue confess the Lord,
and acknowledge His hand in all things.
						SUN FLOWER.


FAIR AT GLENWOOD. 

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	At Richfield, on the 22nd and 23rd of Sep-
tember, we held our Annual Stake Fair for the
Primary Associations of Sevier County; the
Young Ladies also held theirs with us this year,
which was pronounced a great success. A
pleasant feature of the fair was the work-shop,
where children from six to twelve years, worked
at different work, some sewing, carding wool,
spinning, knitting, braiding straw, carpentering,
etc. It is really surprising to note the advance-
ment the Primary children are making, both
temporal and spiritual, and we feel to thank
God and Sister Eliza R. Snow Smith for the
organization.
	The articles that were contributed are too
numerous to mention; the hand work, baking,
and some very ingenious articles made by the
boys, were really excellent, reflecting great
credit on all. The Young Ladies’ tables were
not so well filled, it being their first fair, yet
the Young Ladies of Glenwood had their table
well-supplied both in fancy work and hand
sewing, for which they certainly deserve much
praise; there were also articles from other wards,
which showed great taste and patience. We
hope the Young Ladies will try again. 
								H.
Glenwood, Sept. 30, 1887.


HOW TO STRENGTHEN THE
				MEMORY

	Thurlow Weed has given a most interesting
account of the method adopted by himself for
strengthening his memory.
	"When a young man," says Mr. Weed, "I
had to adopt a regular method for strengthen-
ing my memory, and I hit on one that was very
effective. I got married in 1818, when I was
working in Albany as a journeyman printer.
In a few months I went into business, establish-
ing a newspaper for myself, and some of my
friends thought I would succeed in politics,
probably because I impressed my views strongly
on those about me. But I saw at once that I
had a fatal weakness which I thought would
prevent it. My memory was a seive; I could
remember nothing. Dates, names, appoint-
ments, faces, everything, escaped me. I said
to my wife, "Catherine, I shall never make a
successful politician. For I cannot remember,
and that is a prime necessity of politicians. A
politician who sees a man once should remem-
bes him forever.' My wife told me I must train
my memory. So when I came home that night
I sat down alone, and spent fifteen minutes try-
ing silently to recall with accuracy the princi-
pal events of the day.  I could remember but
little at first; now I remember that I could not
then recall what I had for breakfast. After a
few days' practice I found I could recall more.
Events came back to me more minutely, more
accurately, and more vividly than at first.
After a fortnight or so of this, Catherine said,
'Why don't you relate to me the events of the
day, instead of recalling them to yourself? It
would be interesting, and my interest in it
would be a stimulus to you.' Having a great
respect for my wife's opinion, I began a habit
of oral confession, as it were, which was con-
tinued for almost fifty years. Every night,
the last thing before retiring, I told her every-
thing I could remember that had happened to
me, or about me during the day. I generally
recalled the very dishes I had had for breakfast,
dinner, and tea; the people I had seen and what
they had said; the editorials I had written for
my paper, giving her brief extracts of them;
I mentioned all the letters I had sent or re-
ceived. and the very language used, as nearly
as possible, when I had walked or ridden.
told her everything that had come within m


observation. I found I could say my lessons
better and better every year, and instead of
the practice growing irksome, it became a
pleasure to go over again the events of the day.
I am indebted to this discipline for a memory
of somewhat unusual tenacity,and I recommend
the practice to all who wish to store up facts,
or expect to have much to do with influencing
men."
	Scholars, scientists, and others engaged in
active intellectual pursuits, realize how neces-
sary to success is a good memory; and nearly
every person, of whatever mental calibre, from
experience organized into instinct, remembers
the hard facts connected with the daily routine
of work; but how few persons take the trouble
to cultivate the memory as a fine art. The
number of people who forget events, dates, and
names is astonishing. These excuse themselves
by saying, “I did not expect to have occasion
to remember.” The pleasures of memory are
a myth to them, and everything profitless that
will not be required for actual use to-day or to-
morrow. It is from this class that we recruit-
that vast army of people whose memories
though confessedly defective and untrained in
historical and literary matter, have always per-
fect recollections of damaging incidents and
facts concerning a friend's misfortunes or short
comings. In repeating a story about A, B, or
C, they resent as an insult the suggestion of
possible inaccuracies and crudeness.
	“Oh, no! I remember perfectly all about it,”
when, in fact, they remember nothing but a few
confused fragments, out of which they recon-
struct an entirely new series of facts and inci-
dents. The most important point in strengthen-
ing the memory is not that incidents and occur-
rences of minor consequence may be carried in
the mind for years; because the memory, like a
storehouse, can easily be overcrowded with
mental rubbish, but that the pith of one's daily
experience and learning may be impressed upon
the brain and become part of its structure. In
this way only can one add to substantial knowl-
edge day by day, and lay the foundation for
ready understanding, keen perception, acute
judgment, and quick wit.
	Said a lawyer friend: “I make it a point
never to remember a word of what I said in
any particular case; what I remember are the
facts, the mental research, the emotions, facul-
ties, feelings called into play by that case. and
from these recollections, which are the meat
within the shell, I am able to argue more and
more forcibly each time in similar cases.”
	This faculty for discarding the chaff and re-
taining the wheat of experience is to be ac-
quired only by persistent education and train-
ing of the memory to seize upon and appropri-
ate the substance of events and incidents and
not the verbiage.
	Begin in the school-room by educating
children to repeat, not the words, but the sub-
stance contained in the lesson of the text-book;
and the habits of thought and memory (and
not parrot-like utterance) will very probably
become fixed for a life-time.
						—Dress.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿


MARGARET FULLER.

	The following anecdote of Margaret Fuller,
which we believe has not before been printed,
comes from  correspondent of the Critic, in
Boston:
	“One evening Miss Fuller, with a young
friend, took a suburban train to attend a small
social gathering. She had dressed with unusu-
al care, the finishing touch being a bunch of
beautiful hot-house flowers, which she wore in
her corsage. When the conductor came into
the car to collect the fares Margaret suddenly
remembered that her pocket-book was in the
dress she had left at home. Her companion


was in the same plight as herself and there
was nothing to be done but to explain matters
to the conductor, That official proved obdur-
ate, however; the ladies were both strangers
to him, and their excuse was hackneyed and
transparent; he would be obliged to leave them
at the next way-station. Snatching the bouquet
from beneath her cloak, Margaret rose to her
feet with queen like dignity and exclaimed:
'Is there no one in this car who will purchase
these flowers?’ A gentleman instantly came
forward and paid the fares of the two ladies,
but, it is needless to say refused to take the
bouquet. The card which Margaret exchanged
with him, however, he keeps to this day, and
the incident was the beginning of a pleasant
acquaintance between them.”


﻿NOTES AND NEWS.

	Miss Emily Faithful, writing on the coming
conference to consider the industrial position of
women, urges that three points must be in-
sisted upon:—First  The need of definite
training for girls. Second. The best work,
at fair market price must be given. Third.
Social prejudices must be abandoned, and
ladies must be encouraged to go into business.

	“I wonder what my great-grandfather
would say to me if he could come back to
earth,” said a young lady lawyer to a news-
paper reporter recently. “He was so angry
with my grandmother when he discovered that
she had gone beyond the four fundamental
rules in arithmetic, that he kept her shut up
for a week on a diet of bread and water, and
commanded her to let all books alone forever,
except the Bible. He thought it a disgrace,
poor man, that a daughter of his should be
willing to pursue such masculine studies, and
when she confessed that she liked to do sums
in the Rule of Three, he felt justified in resort-
ing to severe measures to check such unnatural
propensities. If he could see two of his great-
granddaughters practicing medicine and one
aspiring to law, he would think that grand-
mother’s love of study had broken out in a
worse form in this generation. What ghostly
flagellations he would bestow!” and she
laughed, shrugging her shoulders.

	According to Prof. Brinton, of the Academy
of Natural science, many important traces of
early man are to be discovered in the Mis-
sissippi Valley. Near St. Paul begins the modi-
fied glacial drift of an intermediate glacial
period. Fifteen feet below the surface in it are
found stone implements and remains of work-
shops. In Patagonia remains of fires, tools,
and implements of bone are found. These
things indicate a somewhat advanced stage of
civilization and were left by men who lived
here before the horse was extinct. From these
things it is inferred that a race, with race types
and characteristics, existed here as early, if
not earlier, than elsewhere on the globe. The
characteristics of the American race is color
ranging from copper to white; stiff hair and
little of it; foreheads retreating, compressed at
the sides, and low, eyes straight, noses dissimilar,
mouths large, chin round, small and regular,
expression hard and unpleasant. The sexes
are much alike in appearance, when they do
not wear garments fashioned for the purpose of
distinguishing them. The higher the develop-
ment of man, the more pronounced is the dis-
tinction between the sexes.

Vol. 16			SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, NOVEMBER 1, 1887.		No. 11.


FEAR NOT, ZION.

Fear not, Zion ! countless hosts
	Help thy cause behind the vail;
Zion's friends are uppermost,
	Zion's Founder will not fail;
God His purpose will fullfill,
God is Zion’s refuge still.

Fear not, Zion ! steadfast be;
	Yet a little while endure;
Wouldst thou triumph and be free
	Let thine aims be high and pure,
	"Love of righteousness" must dwell
In the hearts of Israel.

Fear not, Zion!  watch and pray—
	In the midst of cares and strife—
While ye toil, as best ye may,
	For the needs of mortal life-
Watch! "for all who run may read"—
That “the end' is near indeed. 

What if woes beset the just,
	Thick as sparks that upward fly? 
Fear not, Zion! put thy trust
	Aiways in the Lord, Most High;
Saved from many a grief and snare,
All who cast on God their care.

Powers of darkness needs must rage;
	Dire "offenses" try the soul;
Fierce the warfare all must wage,
	Who would reach salvation's goal—
Rough "the way," but who can sum, 
All their bliss who overcome?
	
Fear not, Zion ! onward press,
	 Matchless thy Redeemer’s power;
Weak the foes of righteousness—
	Frail as e’en a fading flower;
Seem they now a mighty host?
‘Tis but human strength they boast.

Fear not, Zion ! Lo, the dawn
	Soon shall bid the shadows flee;
Tribulation leadeth on
	To immortal victory;
Fear not, Zion ! come what will,
God will guide His people still.
				EMILY H. WOODMANSEE.
Salt Lake City.


MATER DOLOROSA.
		(MOURNING, MOTHER).

Because of little low-laid heads all crowned
		With golden hair,
Forever more all fair young brows to me
		A halo wear:
I kiss them reverently—alas!  I know
 		The stains I bear.

Because of dear, but close-shut holy eyes
		Of heaven's own blue,
All little, eyes do fill my own with tears,
		Whate’er their hue;
And motherly I gaze their innocent
		Clear depths into.

Because of little pallid lips that once
		My name did call,
No childish voice, in vain appeal, upon
		My ear doth fall.
I count it all my joy their joys to share,
		And sorrows small.	

Because of little dimpled cherished hands,
		Which folded lie,
All little hands henceforth to me do have
		A pleading cry;
   clasp them as they were small wandering birds
		Lured home to fly.


Because of little death-cold feet, for earth's
		Rough roads unmeet,
I'd journey leagues to save from sin or harm
		Such little feet;
And count the lowliest service done for them
		So sacred—sweet!
							MARY K. FIELD.


TRAINING CHILDREN.

	"The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and he that
winneth souls is wise."—PROVERBS, xii, 30.

	Many reflections pass through my mind with
regard to our youths, who are growing up in
Zion.  I can see that great responsibilities rest
upon parents in the way they train their child-
ren, from the time they can understand a look
or a word. Their mind from birth may be
compared to a sheet of white paper, susceptible
to impressions made, good or evil.
	As I understand, this instruction to be made
upon the child’s mind by the parent, is obedi-
ence to their commands. The imperative “no,”
should be given to its wrong acts, and the ac-
quiescive “yes,” to its right ones.  A frown
should rest upon the countenance of the parent
to show it is in the wrong, and a smile to show
approbation. To you young mothers, let me
say this is a very easy way to govern your
children, without hard words or blows, and
never threaten to punish unless you put that
into execution, and then not in anger, or for
slight cause, but with calm and decisive words,
in reason, showing the wrong. Many a child is
ruined by wrong tuition, and it is high time
that we understand true government, first to
govern ourselves.  Of all people we should
practice that which will result to benificent ends.
The child should be taught not to destroy things.
This evil is a growing-propensity in our child-
ren; teach them the use and value of everything;
when they begin to cry for things that they
should not break to pieces, cross them in their
temper, and show them your superior judgment;
teach them to be clean in their habits, and this
will grow with their growth, and make you a
great deal less trouble.
	And, dear sisters, never counteract the com-
mands of your husbands to your children, but
teach them to obey and respect him and his
word. What I have suggested here can begin
 to be taught to your child before it can walk or
talk.  When they are old enough to work
teach them industrious habits; keep the body
well balanced with the mind; activity brings
health, strength and wealth; teach them order;
this will make labor easy, and lay a foundation
to build the character upon, and their moral
lives. “Train up a child in the way it should
go, and when it is old it will not depart from
it.”
	Let us not form these vague ideas that our
children cannot accomplish what we have done
before them, and have surmounted, barriers
that will fall in their way. While they are
full of life and vigor, labor will strengthen
their more noble instincts and their minds to be
led to fill a life of usefulness, and not idleness,
that leads to crime and misuse of time.  What
a pleasing sensation fills our minds when we
enter a house where there is union and a well
disciplined family, each doing their part, and
all striving to make one another happy.
	Here is where our heaven is to begin, in the
family circle.  If we see this order of things at
our neighbor’s, it only gratifies us for a short

[Column 2]
time if we have it not in our own. Then let
us all strive together to have happy homes,
lovely and obedient children, not be selfish,
teach them to be humane and generous, to take
every burden off the shoulders of the aged and
infirm. And you young boys from ten to four-
teen years of age, four or five get together
every tenth day and clean out all those obnox-
ious burdocks and weeds in the streets, pile
them in the middle of the street and burn
them up. You can pay tithing that way, or
poll tax; and, oh, what a satisfaction it would
be to see our streets made clean and tidy, and
poor widows’ gardens cleaned up, and your
own.
	If we will give attention to all these things,
soon, very soon “Zion will become the joy of
the whole earth,” and the fir tree and the box-
wood will be brought up to beautify the home
of the Saints.
	Please read in Societies.
					MARY ANN M. PRATT.
Oct. 16, 1887.


﻿MORALITY.

	It is generally conceded to those who have
made the subject a study, that the nature of 
man is made of three distinct parts viz.: the 
physical, the intellectual, and the moral.  The 
physical constitutes the material part of man's
nature, and the intellectual is made manifest
through the operations of the mind.  It is to 
the development of these two that much time,
money, and thought are devoted. We hear a 
great deal said, from time to time, about edu-
cation. The little boy is told, that if he will
study hard, while young to gain an education,
the chances are, that, at some future day, he
will be a great man.  He is told that if he
study hard enough he will; probably, fill the
position of president of the United States.
	The little girls, too, are shown no less glit-
tering pictures of future advantages that await
them; on conditions that they are diligent in
acquiring a knowledge of a prescribed number
of branches taught in school.  Not only are 
parents anxious that their little ones grow up 
with all the scholastic advantages which they
can afford to give them, but it is with equal solici-
tude that they watch with a jealous care that
no accident befall them, or anything from in-
fancy up to maturity, happen to them which
is liable to injure or mar, in the least, the
physical growth and perfection of their chil-
dren.  It is, undoubtedly, a source of intense
pleasure for parents to contemplate the beau-
tiful and well formed bodies of their offspring
and to see the fruits of their cultivated minds
made manifest.  This, in a measure rewards
them for all their watchfulness and trouble.
All these provisions for the development of the
rising generation, both mentally and physical-
ly are very good and as they should be, but
there is still another education which should 
receive even more attention than these named
and without which, an education, in the gener-
al sense of the term, often becomes a curse in-
stead of a blessing to its possessor. This educa-
tion consists in the culture and development of
the third nature of man known as morality
and which directs the other two in a right
course.  It is that course of action which con-
science dictates as the right course.  It is
that which would guides one to do his duty to
God, to his fellow beings, to himself, and to
the lower animals.  It is that course of ac-


tion which conscience dictates as right
and which has been expressed in the words, “Do
unto others as you would have them do unto
you.”
	We are all aware that the persons who are
capable of doing the greatest amount of injury
or the darkest crimes, in the home, the village,
the state or the government, have been edu-
cated, so far as intellectual culture is concerned;
but search their private records and they have
been found almost invariably, to be persons of 
little or no moral culture. To neglect this
branch of education of a child, is to sew the
seeds of crime and vice, and to allow them to
take their lessons, in morality, from vicious as-
sociates upon the street corners and disrepu-
table places is equally as bad. As a people, we
believe that our future glory, will, in a great
measure, depend on our children, and it is
sad neglect of a duty when we allow their
moral natures to become corrupted.  It should
be the object of parents and children, to de-
velop in the highest degree, morality; to seek
every means to promote its growth, and en-
deavor just as earnestly for thier education in
this as they do to develop the physical and intel-
lectual nature.  And as the moral is the con-
trolling power over the other two, and that
which is to decide the entire character; it is im-
perative, in order to make a good member of
society, that the utmost care and attention be
given to moral culture, and its most perfect de-
velopment.			L.A. SCHOFIELD.
	Nephi.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿WOMEN OF INDIA.
			MOHAMMEDANS.
	In India all Mohammedan women are “pur-
dah women”—purdah meaning a curtain or
screen. As they say, they “keep purdah."
They live behind the curtain, and their faces
are seen by no man, except the husband, father,
or brothers—not even by the husband’s brothers,
unless they are young boys. They occupy
that portion of the house which is known as
the “zenana”—the place of the women. All
Mohammedan houses are similiarly constructed.
A large building in front, richly furnished, for
the men; behind this an open court with foun-
tains, dwarf palm trees, etc and back of the
court the meagerly furnished zenana.
	The patriarchal system is kept up as with
the Hindoos, the son and grandsons bringing
their wives to their father’s house, and three or
four generations are usually found under one
roof. The women spend all their time in the
zenana. They gossip and smoke, and array
themselves in their fine clothing and jewelry,
and no doubt quarrel a bit sometimes.
	A wife is allowed to dine at her mothers’s
house once a month. If her family is wealthy
she covers herself to the eyes in a large chud-
dah, and is taken to the house in a closed car-
riage, or palanquin. If she is poor, she wraps
the chuddah about her and walks to the house
at midnight, when the streets are empty, re-
turning the next night at the same hour.
	On my first visit to a zenana, early in my
Bombay life, I went with an interpreter, for I
had not learned Hindoostani and could not
understand one word of conversation in that
language. At the first house we found all the
women in one room, and they appeared to be
entertaining a figure which was shrouded in a
white sheet, and was sitting upon a large, white
cushion or mattress, which was spread upon the
floor.
	Upon inquiry, I learned that this ghostly
shrouded figure was a newly made widow, her
husband having died at sea two weeks before.
They said that she must never leave the cushion,
upon the floor for a single moment, until her
period of mourning—four and one-half months
—was past.
[Column 2]
	The windows of the hot room must be kept
tightly closed, and the blinds drawn down, lest
some man in some other house should see her.
If, on the very last day of the four and a half
months, the shadow of a man should fall upon
her, she must commence over again, and sit
four and a half months longer. On this ac-
count, the widow is carefully guarded until her
period of mourning is ended. If the house is
large and she has a mother to wait upon her,
it is easily managed. But if they are poor
and the family all live in one room, as is often
the case, the mourning widow’s lot is a hard
one. They partition off one corner of the
room with matting, make a little pen for her,
and in that close, stuffy place she must stay
four and a half months.
	Formerly Mohammedan girls were married
nearly as young as the Hindoos, but now they
do not marry until they are eleven or twelve
years old.  They say it is too hard to keep
little girl widows sitting so long.
	The women of the house I visited were doing
their utmost to cheer and entertain the widow,
and help her to pass away the time.
	When female relatives came from outside,
there was alwaws a certain amount of wailing
and beating of breasts, but it soon developed
into an afternoon gossip.
	As a rule, a Mussulmani woman is not too
much attached to her husband. She never sees
him until the day of the wedding, and often
does not know whether she is marrying a man
of her own age, or an old gray-headed villain,
who has buried several wives.
	At the next house I visited, I found four
wives of one man—a sheik who had been a
grand man in Zanzibar before coming to Bom-
bav. He first married an Abyssinian woman,
who died while he was at Zanzibar. He saw a
good many English ladies there whom he ad-
mired, and he determined that his second wife
should be white. So he sent to Constantinople
for a Circassian girl. She was pretty, and he
was, so well pleased with her that he soon sent
for another, and again another. At about the
same time the Sultan of Muscat died, leaving a
pretty widow who was also a Circassian or
Georgian, and the old sheik took her for the
fourth wife, making his number complete, as
the koran allows four wives to all faithful fol-
lowers of the prophet, who can support so
many.
	This was a very happy family, the wives
calling themselves sisters. The eldest boy,
when speaking of the family, said: "My father
is well and my four mothers are well.” These
four mothers took very good care of their nine
children, and each seemed equally anxious when
one was ill.
	In the course of time, the old sheik died,
and then it became a serious question how the
widows could keep their mourning period, as
there was no female relative to look after them.
Finally a friend of the family, an Arab lady,
came to the rescue and took them all home to
her own large house, where there was plenty of
room to conceal them. The childrem were al-
lowed to visit them once a day,so their mourn-
ing period was very comfortably passed.
	On a man’s tomb there is a representation of
a pen box, on his wife’s a blank tablet. This
is to show that a woman’s mind is blank, except
as her husband writes upon it. They do grant
a woman a soul, but it is of an inferior kind,
aud there will be a special place prepared for
her, outside her husband’s paradise.
	The dead are buried in a sitting posture, so
that they may the better fight the angel, when
he comes to question them of “the deeds done
in the body." A woman who never marries
is worshipped as a saint, and all her relatives
prostrate themselves before her and kiss her
feet. 
	If a woman is ill, an old "hakim" is con-
sulted. He writes a text from the koran on a
[Column 3]	
slip of paper, and directs her to soak the paper
in a glass of water and take the resulting medi-
cine in divided doses. This she does religiously,
in full confidence that she will be cured.
	In the thirteenth century, a Mohammedan
woman named Rezia ruled all Hindoostan. She
was beautiful and clever, and went about ad-
ministering justice among men, as if she her-
self were, a man. A Turki chief rebelled
against her. There was a severe battle and
Rezia was defeated, but she soon conquered her
conqueror, by marrying him.
	About 1550, Chand Bibi was reigning queen
of Ahmednagar, her husband, the nizam, hav-
ing died before their son was old enough to be
crowned in his stead. During her reign, the
fort of Ahmednagar was besieged and a breach
made in the wall. Chand Bibi led the army in
person, and defended the breach until assistance
came from her brother in Bijapore and Ahmed-
nagar was saved. She wore a vail over her
face, and was known to the troops as “The Lady
of the Vail.” On a high hill near the city,
there stands to-day a beautiful structure known
as “Chand Bibi’s Mahal.” It was built by a
grateful people to perpetuate the memory of a
queen who saved her country. Every one
knows the story of the Emperor Shah Jihan’s
favorite wife, Nur Mahal, or “The Light of
the World,” as she is often called, and the
glorious Taj at Agra, which he built for her
tomb. There it stands on the banks of the
Jumna, as fair and white to-day as when it was
finished, more than two hundred and fifty years
ago. It has been fitly called, “A poem in
marble” Edwin Arnold says of it in “India
Re-visited:” “The crown of the world, the
peerless tomb built for the fair dead body of
Nur Mahal, by her lorn and lover, the Emperor
Shah Jihan In all the world, no queen had
ever such a monument. 		*	*	*	*	
	As you pass beneath the stately portal, in itself
sufficient to commemorate the proudest of
princesses, and as the white cupola of the Taj
rises before the gaze and reveals its beauty,
grace by grace, the mind refuses to criticise
what delights the eye and fills the heart with
reverence for the royal love which could thus
translate itself into alabaster. The moral of
this tomb is written near by: ‘Saith Jesus (on
whom be peace), this world is a bridge; pass
thou over it, but build not upon it. This
world is one hour; give its minutes to thy
prayers; for the rest is unseen.’ ”
	The Begum of Bhopal is the only reigning
Mohammedan woman, at this time. Bhopal is
one of the smaller of the native states, but it
has been so wisely managed that it brings in a
large revenue. During the Sepoy mutiny, the
present begum’s mother ruled the state. She
remained loyal to the British government, and
offered men and money for subduing the re-
bellion.
	The present begum has received two decora-
tions by order of the queen empress—“The
Companion of the Star of India,” and one
other which is conferred upon women only.
			—S. F. Norris in Hearth and Hall.


﻿The Pundita Ramabi has brought out a
second edition of her deeply-interesting book,
“High-Caste Hindu Woman.” Every woman
ought to read it.

	The temperance women of England have
been getting up a jubilee memorial to the
Queen in the shape of a petition that the bar-
rooms be closed on Sunday. It now contains
three-quarters of a million signatures.

	Vittoria Colonna was boried in a small and
obscure church in Rome, now being demolished,
Sant’ Anna dei Falegnami; and the archaeolo-
gists and literary people of that city are anxi-
ously expecting the discovery of her “cypress
wood coffiin, lined with embroidered velvet.”


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ROMOLA.

			BY A. C. BOWERS

A poet’s vision, clad in the fair guise 
	Of a lily, all in white and gold—
	Hers not the form for passionate arms to fold;
She loves, but loves in such angelic wise
As might some wanderer from the upper skies,
	Who wears, with rosy lips of tenderest curve, 
The starry purity of saintly eyes.
	But if some lofty purpose were to serve,
The fair and delicate figure then would seem
	One who could w lk, with straight unshaken tread.
	The flaming city of the unpardoned dead
(Shown to the Florentine in lurid dream),
	Serene and Scatheleess thro’ the infernal glow—
	Nor touch of fire upon her raiment know.
				—New Orleans Times-Democrat.


TO PATRIARCH L. D. YOUNG. 

		OCTOBER 19TH, 1887. 

Dear Uncle, once again we come, 
	With loving hearts to greet you, 
And find you still in this dear home, 
	Where 'tis so good to meet you. 

How many in the passing year
	Have gone to other places; 
Some honored much, some very dear, 
	Whose names fond memory traces. 

Imprisoned, exiled, sank and died, 
	And thus escaped each jailer,
Our faithful President and guide,
	The valiant, brave John Taylor. 

Your nephew, F. M. Little, too, 
	So kind and so attentive, 
Has also passed to regions new—
	The world held no preventive. 

And, shall I mention your dear boy, 
	Your Brigham?  whose transition
Brings tears, not all of grief, we joy
	That he fulfilled his mission. 

And faithful to each covenant
	He'd made before high heaven,
Was satisfied and well content
	With th' last summons given. 

To-day, last tributes of respeet,
	To one of Zion's numbers—
Long years her leading architect—
	Truman O. Angel slumbers. 

But let me not too long retain
	Your mind on heavy crosses, 
To each of these we count great gain, 
	We only bear the losses. 

And you still live and battle on, 
	With cares both light and weighty, 
While years and years have pass'd and gone, 
	Till yours have numbered eighty. 

How long, how very long to live, 
	And yet you seem so sprightly, 
I often wonder if we give
	Your dates and figures rightly. 

The last of all your mother's sons,
	Her daughters all have left us;
How many, many faithful ones
	Have gone, and thus bereft us!

Why is it that my thoughts will turn
	To those who dwell above us? 
Perhaps because our hearts so yearn
	For those we love—who love us. 

Now, should I pause and try again
	Of sadder things I'm fearful;
And so will lay aside the pen—
	Let's talk of something cheerful. 
								LULA.


﻿D. M. MULOCK CRAIK.

	Mrs. Dinah Maria Mulock Craik who died in
England, on the 13th of October, 1887, was
born at Stoke upon Trent in 1826. Next to
George Eliott as an authoress in modern times
her name must stand. Her books have a very
refined character, taken as a whole. She has
studied human nature and woman's nature
pretty thoroughly. Her books are as popular
in America as in England, but not more so.
Her first work of fiction that attracted atten-
tion, and it is said to be her first attempt at
romance was entitled, “The Ogilvies,” she was
then 23 years of age. The next year she pub-
lished  "Olive;” at the age of 25 “The Head of
the Family;” shortly after “Agatha’s Hus
band.” and from that time on has written vol-
ume after volume. “John Halifax, Gentle-
man” is considered by most people her best
work; though I greatly prefer some others; her
women are generally well drawn and strong
minded,her men weaker and yet, the newspaper
critics, some of them say, she makes women too
submissive and subservient. To me she seems
to have the best ideas of woman, women must
give if they expect to receive, must honor if
they expect to be honored. Miss Mulock’s
“Hannah” is the best argument of the injustice
of the old English law against marriage with
a deceased wife’s sister that I have ever seen
and ought to have as much weight with the
English people and Parliament in the annull-
ing of that law as “Uncle Tom’s Cabin” had
in the United States in abolishing slavery.
	Among literary people Mrs. Craik was a
great favorite though I know very little of her
except from her writings. To me she seems
a woman of wonderful imaginative capacity
in character upright, straight-forward, con-
scientious, appreciative and honest. Well
acquainted with English life in its realty, I
hope some day to see in print a sketch of her
home life written by one who would do her
ample justice—ED.
			THE FUNERAL.
	The funeral of Mrs. Craik, best known as
Miss Mulock, took place in London, Oct. 16th.
Her mortal remains rest in a corner angle of
the Keston churchyard, not far from the grave
of Darwin.
	When the family cortege left the pretty
vine-embowered villa built of red tile, Gothic
style, where she had so long lived with her hus-
band (a partner in Macmillan & Co’s), there
was brilliant sunshine; but when the bier, borne
upon the shoulders of six gentleman friends of
the family, entered the old-fashioned church a
shower of rain gently fell, to be followed by
genial sunshine, when the service—which the
vicar performed—was ended.
	A PICTURESQUE GRAVE.
	The grave presented a picturesque appear-
ance, its sides and bottom being in pure white
chalky soil surrounded with exquisite flowers,
from wreaths sent by order of the Queen and
by Lord Tennyson, and other floral designs
which were sent by relatives and friends, to the
modest gifts of wild flowers from Sunday
School children aud rural neighbors. There
was a large attendance—of some who came to
honor themselves by paying their respect to the
memory of the novelist, poet and essayist, and
of throngs of neighbors who chiefly recognized
in her loss that of a good friend and benevolent
lady whom all the parish had learned to love.
	SOME OF THE MOURNERS.
Among the mourners were the brothers Mac-
millan and their families; John Morley, the
statesman and author; Holman Hunt. R. A.,
and wife; Sir George Grove, the great musical
critic; J. Norman Lockyer, the poet; Henry
Craik, O. B., and family; Frederick Greenwood,
[Column 2]
the journalist, and Mary Anderson, who placed
upon the coffin a basket of exquisite exotics.
I must not forget Mrs. Craik’s adopted daughter,
whose banus were read in church only last
Sunday in the presence of Mrs. Craik. During
a hedge-side walk, years ago, the authoress
found a babe that had been abandoned. Her
innocent smiles and coquettish ways immedi-
ately won the heart of her future mother, who
adopted and reared her.
		HER OWN HYMN.
	The village choir, as the coffin was lowered,
sang a hymn which it was said Miss Mulock
had written, one verse of which I send, as fol-
lows:—
		"Earth to earth and dust to dust.
			Calmly now thy words we say.
		Left behind, we wait in trust 
			For the Resurrection Day.
		Father, in thy gracious keeping
			Leave we now thy servant sleeping."

		HER ILLNESS AND DEATH.
	I learned concerning the circumstances of
her death that Dr Phillips, of Bromley—a
village adjacent to Shortlands, where she lived,
had been in attendance upon Mrs. Craik for
sometime, although her health had not been So
bad as to occasion serious concern to her
friends.
	But there is reason to believe that she her-
self suspected an affection of the heart. About
a quarter to five on Wednesday afternoon last
she was taken suddenly ill in her bedroom and
fell down, to die in a few minutes.
		THE VICAR’S TRIBUTE.
	The vicar, in a brief address, seems to have
well summed up Mrs. Craik’s mental character-
istics when he said:
	“To exquisite charm of style, ingenuity of
plot and original power of humor and pathos
she added a keen insight into the workings of
the human heart aud a quick conception of
high ideas of life and duty.”—Cablegram to the
New York Herald.


Signora Crispi, the wife of the Italian
Minister, is much interested in the education
of her countrywomen. Fully understanding
how useful a friendly intercourse with foreign
ladies would be, she means to establish in Rome
an international Ladies' Club, where women of
all nations can meet. A reading room will be
open and lectures given, and discussions may
be held of the means to establish industrial
schools for women and technical instruction.

	Jennv Lind lives in London in a house
covered with vines and surround-
ed by trees and flowers. She is a confirmed in-
valid, and very rarely sees callers. To all
Americans who knock at her door to pay their
respects, the servant, who has lived with her
for the past thirty years, delivers the message
that "Jenny Lind wishes them to say that she
will never cease to love the American people
with all her heart." She has just had a stroke
of general paralysis, which, however, has left
her brain unimpaired.

	Miss Phebe Couzins has taken the usual oath
of office, and is regularly installed as city mar-
shall of St Louis. She found no difficulty in
obtaining bondsmen for $20,000. The back sal-
ary due her father, of $2,500, has been paid to her.
Miss Couzins has the distinction of being the
first woman to hold this office. It is reported
that Miss Couzins said to President Hayes, just
after his inauguration that it was a pity some
woman lawyer like herself was not the chief
justice who administered the oath to the Presi-
dent. "In that case," gallantly replied "Mr.
Hayes,"I should have kissed, not the book, but
the chief justice."
